title: The Middle East and Global Energy Markets – Topics - IEA
gist: The IEA describes how the Middle East war (begun 28 February) and the resulting blockage of the Strait of Hormuz have caused the largest oil supply disruption in market history, forcing the biggest-ever emergency oil stock release and leaving Brent, product markets, LNG, and adjacent commodities (fertiliser, aluminium, sulphur) all structurally exposed for years to come.

themes.theme: Historic Supply Shock and Emergency Response

insights.claim: The war that began on 28 February has impeded energy trade flows through the Strait of Hormuz, creating the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Investors should treat this as a regime-defining shock rather than a transient geopolitical scare, since IEA itself frames it as unprecedented.
insights.locator: The disruption to oil and gas flows through the Strait of Hormuz

insights.claim: The IEA's Executive Director said the combined impacts of the conflict amount to the greatest threat to global energy security in history.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A top-level official framing of 'worst ever' signals policymakers see asymmetric downside risk, supporting continued strategic reserve intervention and elevated risk premia.
insights.quote: the combined impacts amount to "the greatest threat to global energy security in history."
insights.locator: IEA's Executive Director has said the combined impacts

insights.claim: On 11 March, IEA member countries unanimously agreed to release 400 million barrels of emergency oil stocks, the largest-ever coordinated release in the Agency's history and only the sixth such action ever, following prior releases in 1991, 2005, 2011, and twice in 2022.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The scale and rarity of this release (bigger than Gulf War, Katrina, Libya, or 2022 Russia-related releases) is itself a market signal of severity, and depletes the buffer available for any subsequent shock.
insights.locator: On 11 March, IEA Member countries unanimously agreed

insights.claim: Global observed oil stocks have declined by 3.8 million barrels per day on average since the war started, including a 143 million barrel draw in May alone, the fastest pace of inventory depletion on record.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Record inventory draws mean underlying physical tightness is worse than the pulled-back Brent price suggests, a bullish underlying signal even as headline futures ease.
insights.locator: supply losses stemming from the Middle East disruptions have depleted

insights.claim: Brent futures rose more than 60% above pre-conflict levels at their late-April peak before pulling back in late May and early June as strategic stock releases, weaker demand, and a US-Iran interim agreement eased market tightness.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The pullback was driven by policy intervention and a fragile diplomatic truce rather than a fundamental resolution of the supply problem, meaning the rally could resume if the agreement falters.
insights.locator: Brent futures – the global benchmark for crude prices – rose steeply

insights.claim: Even as Brent futures pulled back, physical crude markets continued to reflect acute supply tightness as refiners scrambled to replace lost Middle Eastern cargoes with alternative supplies, keeping diesel, jet fuel and LPG prices elevated.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A gap between softening flat-price futures and still-tight physical/product markets suggests refined product cracks (diesel, jet, LPG) may be a more durable trade than the crude benchmark itself.
insights.locator: Physical crude markets continue to reflect acute supply tightness

insights.claim: Jet fuel prices nearly tripled following the loss of Middle Eastern exports, though aviation activity running well below normal levels has helped ease some of that pressure.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Demand destruction in aviation is partially self-correcting the jet fuel shock, so watch for a rebound in jet cracks if air travel normalises before Gulf refining capacity is restored.
insights.locator: Aviation activity is also running well below normal levels

themes.theme: The Strait of Hormuz as the Binding Constraint

insights.claim: The IEA states that fully resuming flows through the Strait of Hormuz remains the single most important variable in easing pressure on energy supplies, prices and the global economy.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: This is the master variable to track for any resolution trade; all other mitigations (stock releases, rerouting, non-Middle East output) are described as partial substitutes at best.
insights.quote: fully resuming flows through the Strait of Hormuz remains the single most important variable in easing the pressure on energy supplies, prices and the global economy.
insights.locator: Our latest monthly Oil Market Report has more

insights.claim: Even if the US-Iran agreement holds, a full recovery in Strait of Hormuz flows is unlikely to be immediate because mines must be cleared from the main shipping lanes and supply chains need time to normalise.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Investors should expect a prolonged, gradual restoration timeline rather than a snap-back in flows and prices even after a ceasefire headline.
insights.locator: even if the new agreement holds, a full recovery in flows is unlikely

insights.claim: Only Saudi Arabia and the UAE have operational crude pipelines able to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, offering an estimated 3.5 to 5.5 million barrels per day of alternative capacity, while Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar and Bahrain have no such alternative and rely on the Strait for the vast majority of their oil exports; about 25% of the world's seaborne oil trade transited the Strait in 2025.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: There is a hard structural ceiling on how much of the disrupted volume can ever be rerouted, meaning several producers are effectively hostage to the Strait reopening regardless of price incentives.
insights.locator: Only Saudi Arabia and the UAE have operational crude pipelines

insights.claim: Shipments of oil through the Strait increased sharply in early June, supported by ship-to-ship transfers in the Gulf of Oman, showing the market adapting workarounds even before a full resolution.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Ship-to-ship transfer activity is a real-time proxy investors can watch via AIS data to gauge de facto flow recovery ahead of official reopening.
insights.locator: Shipments of oil through the Strait increased sharply in early June

insights.claim: Almost 90% of the total oil, product and LNG volumes exported via the Strait of Hormuz in 2025 was destined for Asia, with just over 10% going to Europe.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Asian economies and importers bear the overwhelming brunt of this disruption, making Asian demand destruction and gas rationing the more sensitive macro variable than European exposure.
insights.locator: About 80% of oil and oil products transiting the Strait in 2025

themes.theme: Natural Gas and LNG: A Multi-Year Supply Setback

insights.claim: The Ras Laffan facility in Qatar, the world's largest LNG liquefaction facility, has been offline since an attack on 2 March, cutting Qatar and UAE LNG supply routed through the Strait by over 300 million cubic metres per day, equivalent to more than 2 billion cubic metres of gas per week.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Loss of the single largest liquefaction facility globally is a supply-side shock with no quick substitute, underpinning sustained upward pressure on global gas benchmarks.
insights.locator: The Ras Laffan facility in Qatar

insights.claim: As of mid-June, Dutch TTF, the European benchmark for natural gas prices, stood 35% above pre-war levels, reversing a market-rebalancing trend that had been underway during the 2025/26 heating season.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The conflict has undone a prior easing trend in European gas, meaning positioning built around a rebalancing gas market needs to be reassessed.
insights.locator: As of mid-June, Dutch TTF, the European benchmark

insights.claim: Damage to Qatar's LNG liquefaction infrastructure is expected to delay the anticipated global LNG supply wave, with a cumulative loss of around 120 bcm of LNG supply between 2026 and 2030, and impacts likely to be felt through 2026 and 2027 even though new liquefaction projects elsewhere should eventually offset the losses.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: This directly undercuts the widely anticipated 2026-2027 global LNG glut narrative, implying tighter and higher gas prices for longer than pre-conflict consensus expected.
insights.locator: damage to LNG liquefaction infrastructure in Qatar is set to reduce

insights.claim: Natural gas prices in Asian markets have risen sharply as buyers compete for a smaller pool of LNG cargoes, reflecting the region's greater exposure to Strait of Hormuz disruptions, prompting gas rationing in some countries.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Physical gas rationing is a demand-destruction signal worth monitoring as a leading indicator of how much further Asian LNG demand can absorb before economic damage forces policy responses.
insights.locator: Natural gas prices in Asian markets have risen sharply

themes.theme: Second-Order Contagion into Fertiliser and Metals

insights.claim: More than 30% of global urea trade and about 20% of global ammonia and phosphate trade moves through the Strait of Hormuz, creating risks for food prices and food security, with some countries also using imported LNG to run domestic fertiliser plants, linking the gas shock directly to fertiliser supply.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The Strait disruption is a transmission channel into global food prices via fertiliser inputs, a non-obvious cross-asset link investors in agricultural commodities should track alongside energy.

insights.claim: The Gulf region produces around 8% of global aluminium supply, with about 5 million tonnes shipped annually through the Strait of Hormuz from smelters in Bahrain, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the UAE.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A prolonged Strait closure threatens aluminium supply used across energy technologies and construction, a knock-on industrial metals risk separate from the direct energy story.
insights.locator: the Gulf region produces around 8% of the global supply of aluminium

insights.claim: Around half of global seaborne sulphur trade moves through the Strait of Hormuz, and the resulting sulphuric acid is used not only in fertiliser and chemicals but also in refining petroleum and processing critical minerals such as copper, nickel and zinc.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: This is a hidden supply-chain vector: a Hormuz-driven sulphur shortage could ripple into critical mineral processing and EV/battery supply chains well beyond conventional energy markets.
insights.locator: Around half of global seaborne sulphur trade also moves
